quotes.verbatim_quote: This pattern is based on the scale of the minor seventh chord. It is made up of repeated tones: 1-2-1-2 3-4-3-4 5-6-5-6 7-8-7-8 of the minor seventh chord. Used in this manner, these tones also fit the dominant seventh chord because both chords use the same "parent" scale.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Articulates the shared-parent-scale principle that justifies why one m7 scalar pattern covers both chords of a IIm7-V7 progression, the theoretical bedrock of the chapter's two-measure exercises.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This pattern uses the fragments 3-4-5-7 9-10-9-8 from the scale of the minor seventh chord, and fragment 1-2-6-5 (note the interval between the tones 2 and 6) from the scale of the dominant seventh chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines an asymmetric two-fragment assignment for the IIm7-V7 (two measures) and flags the unusual 2-to-6 interval as a feature the student must consciously hear, not gloss over.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Patterns No. 147-148 will deal with the IIm7-V7-IM7 progression in four measures. Rather than introducing new patterns, we will use two of the patterns previously discussed with the IIm7-V7 progression (two measures) and extend them to the IM7. The student can then apply any of the remaining IIm7-V7 (two measures) progressions to the chords in Patterns No. 147 and 148 and experiment with extending them to the IM7 chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the method's compositional economy: extend mastered two-measure vocabulary into four-measure IM7-containing progressions rather than learning new material.
